---
title: "Network Embedding"
subtitle: "Lecture 08: Advanced Topics in Network Science"
author: "Sadamori Kojaku"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: dark
    slide-number: true
    code-line-numbers: true
    highlight-style: github-dark
    fig-align: center
    smaller: false
    scrollable: false
---

# Network Embedding 🌐

**Task**: Embed a network into a low-dimensional vector space such that:

1. The embedding can recover the network with high accuracy.
2. The embedding should be low-dimensional.

**Question**: How would you do that? What approaches would be viable?

![](figs/graph-embedding-viz.png){fig-align="center" width="80%"}

---

# Pen and Paper ✍️

[Link to exercise](https://tinyurl.com/3ckp36n5)

---

## Idea 1: Network Reconstruction

Compress adjacency matrix $\mathbf{A}$ into a low-dim representation $\mathbf{U}$ that minimizes **the reconstruction error**:

$$
\min \frac{1}{2}||\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{U}\mathbf{U}^\top||_F^2
$$

where

$$
U = \begin{bmatrix}
| & | & & | \\
\mathbf{u}_1 & \mathbf{u}_2 & \cdots & \mathbf{u}_d \\
| & | & & |
\end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}, \quad
\mathbf{u}^\top_i \mathbf{u}_j = 0 \quad \forall i \neq j, \text{ and} \quad \sum_i u_i^2 = 1
$$

**Q**: Try solving this for the 1d case (only $\mathbf{u}_1$).

---

## Proof Sketch

$$
\frac{1}{2}||\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{U}\mathbf{U}^\top||_F^2 = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^N\sum_{j=1}^N (A_{ij} - \lambda u_i u_j)^2
$$

This is a convex function of $u_i$. The gradient is 0 at the minimum. By taking the derivative with respect to $u_i$:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial u_i}\left[ \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^N \sum_{j=1}^N (A_{ij} - \lambda u_i u_j)^2 \right] = 0
$$

---

## Setting the Derivative to Zero

$$
\begin{align}
&2 \times \left[ \sum_{j=1, j\neq i}^N (A_{ij} - \lambda u_i u_j)(-\lambda u_j)\right] +
(A_{ii} - \lambda u_i^2) (-2\lambda u_i) = 0 \\
&\iff -2 \lambda \left[ \sum_{j=1, j\neq i}^N A_{ij}u_j - \lambda^2 u_i u_j^2 \right] - 2 \lambda A_{ii}u_i + 2\lambda^2 u_i^3 \\
&\iff \sum_{j=1}^N A_{ij}u_j - \lambda u_i \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^N u_j^2}_{=1 \text{ (our constraint)}}\\
&\iff \sum_{j=1}^N A_{ij}u_j  = \lambda u_i
\end{align}
$$

This is the **eigenvalue problem** of $\mathbf{A}$:

$$\mathbf{A} \mathbf{u} = \lambda \mathbf{u}$$

**Q**: But we have $N$ eigenvectors with zero gradient. Which one should we choose?

---

## Which Eigenvector Should We Choose?

A guiding criterion is the *reconstruction error*. Choose the eigenvector with eigenvalue $\lambda$ that minimizes:

$$
\frac{1}{2}||\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^\top||_F^2 = \text{Tr}((\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^\top)^\top (\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^\top))
$$

Hint: use the matrix Frobenius norm $||\mathbf{M}||_F^2 = \text{Tr}(\mathbf{M}^\top\mathbf{M})$ and see how it relates to the eigenvalue.

---

## Expanding the Reconstruction Error

$$
\begin{align}
&\text{Tr}((\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^\top)^\top (\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^\top)) \\
&= \underbrace{\text{Tr}(\mathbf{A}^\top\mathbf{A})}_{\text{const.}} - 2\lambda \text{Tr}(\mathbf{u}^\top\mathbf{A}\mathbf{u}) + \lambda^2 \underbrace{\text{Tr}(\mathbf{u}^\top\mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^\top\mathbf{u})}_{\text{const. (why? 🤔)}} \\
&\propto -2 \lambda \underbrace{\text{Tr}(\mathbf{u}^\top\mathbf{A}\mathbf{u})}_{\text{$=\lambda$ (why? 🤔)}}
\end{align}
$$

Altogether, the reconstruction error is:

$$
\frac{1}{2}||\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^\top||_F^2 = - \lambda^2 + \text{const.}
$$

It is minimized when $\lambda$ is **the largest eigenvalue**. So the best $\mathbf{u}$ is the eigenvector corresponding to the largest eigenvalue.

---

## Why Eigenvectors?

![](figs/spectral-decomposition.jpg){fig-align="center"}

---

## Code: Spectral Decomposition

```{python}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "6,7,8"
import igraph as ig
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

A = ig.Graph.Famous("Zachary").get_adjacency_sparse()  # Load the karate club network
eigvals, eigvecs = np.linalg.eig(A.toarray())           # Eigenvalues and eigenvectors
eigvals, eigvecs = np.real(eigvals), np.real(eigvecs)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(15, 3))

for i in range(3):
    u = eigvecs[:, i].reshape((-1, 1))
    lam = eigvals[i]
    basisMatrix = u @ u.T
    sns.heatmap(basisMatrix, ax=axes[i+1], cmap="coolwarm", center=0)
    axes[i+1].set_title(f"Lambda={lam:.2f}")

sns.heatmap(A.toarray(), ax=axes[0], cmap="coolwarm", center=0)
axes[0].set_title("Adjacency Matrix")
plt.show()
```

---

## Solution for General $d$-Dimensional Case

The $d$ eigenvectors associated with the **largest $d$ eigenvalues** give the optimal solution that minimizes the reconstruction error for the $d$-dimensional case.

---

## Coding Exercise 🧑‍💻

```python
import numpy as np
import igraph as ig
A = ig.Graph.Famous("Zachary").get_adjacency_sparse()  # Load the karate club network
```

**Task 1**: Compute the eigenvectors and eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$.

**Task 2**: Compute the sum of reconstructed matrices using the first $d$ eigenvectors for $d = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ and compare with the original $\mathbf{A}$:

$$
\frac{1}{2}\left|\left|\mathbf{A} - \sum_{i=1}^d \lambda_i \mathbf{u}_i \mathbf{u}_i^\top\right|\right|_F^2
$$

where $\mathbf{u}_i$ is the eigenvector corresponding to the $i$-th largest eigenvalue $\lambda_i$.

---

## Idea 2: Graph Cut ✂️

**Graph Cut Problem**: Disconnect a graph into two components by cutting the minimum number of edges:

$$
\min_{Q,S} \text{cut}(Q,S) = \sum_{i \in Q} \sum_{j \in S} A_{ij}, \quad \text{where } Q \cup S \approx N, \quad Q \cap S = \emptyset
$$

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
This has a close relationship with the **spectra** of the network.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](figs/GraphMinCut2-ConductanceHERO.width-1250.png)
:::
:::

---

## Ratio Cut

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
The Ratio Cut penalizes unbalanced partitions:

$$
\sum_{i \in Q} \sum_{j \in S} A_{ij} \left( \frac{1}{|Q|} + \frac{1}{|S|} \right)
$$

**Challenge**: The RatioCut objective can be expressed as:

$$
\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{L} \mathbf{x}
$$

where $\mathbf{L} = \mathbf{D} - \mathbf{A}$ is the combinatorial Laplacian and $\mathbf{x}$ is an indicator vector:

$$
x_i = \begin{cases}
\sqrt{|S|/|Q|} & \text{if } i \in Q \\
-\sqrt{|Q|/|S|} & \text{if } i \in S
\end{cases}
$$

Prove this!
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.callout-note}
**Note**: $x_i$ has convenient properties: **zero mean** and **normalization**:
$$\sum_i x_i = 0, \quad \sum_i x_i^2 = N$$
:::

I'll challenge you with a similar problem (normalized cut) later. Form a group, try to solve it together. Bonus points for solving it during the lecture!
:::
:::

---

## Deriving the Ratio Cut Formula

Consider the distance between points $x_i$ and $x_j$:

$$
(x_i - x_j)^2 = \left(\sqrt{\frac{|S|}{|Q|}} + \sqrt{\frac{|Q|}{|S|}}\right)^2 = \frac{N^2}{|Q||S|}
$$

$$
\begin{align}
\text{RatioCut}(Q,S) &= \sum_{i \in Q} \sum_{j \in S} A_{ij} \left( \frac{|Q| + |S|}{|Q||S|} \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i \in Q} \sum_{j \in S} A_{ij}  \left( x_i - x_j \right)^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{2N}\sum_{i=1}^N  \sum_{j=1}^N A_{ij}  \left( x_i - x_j \right)^2
\end{align}
$$

(The last equality holds because $(x_i - x_j)^2 = 0$ for $i,j \in Q$ or $i,j \in S$.)

---

## Completing the Derivation

By expanding the square term:

$$
\begin{align}
\text{RatioCut}(Q,S) &= \frac{1}{2N}\sum_{i=1}^N  \sum_{j=1}^N A_{ij}  \left( x_i - x_j \right)^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N x^2_i \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^N A_{ij}}_{\text{degree } k_i}  - \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \sum_{j=1}^N A_{ij} x_i x_j \\
&= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \sum_{j=1}^N L_{ij} x_i x_j
\end{align}
$$

where $\mathbf{L}$ is the **Combinatorial Laplacian**:

$$
\mathbf{L} = \begin{bmatrix}
k_1 & -A_{12} & \cdots & -A_{1n} \\
-A_{21} & k_2 & \cdots & -A_{2n} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
-A_{n1} & -A_{n2} & \cdots & k_n
\end{bmatrix}
$$

---

## The Optimization Problem

$$
\min_{\mathbf{x}} \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{L} \mathbf{x}
$$

subject to $\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{1} = 0$, $\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{x} = N$, and $x_i \in \left\{\sqrt{|S|/|Q|},\, -\sqrt{|Q|/|S|}\right\}$.

This is an **NP-hard** problem 😿. But there is a way to get a good suboptimal solution using spectral embedding!

**Core idea**: Relax the discrete constraint by allowing $x_i$ to be any real number, keeping the normalization:

$$
\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{1} = 0, \quad \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{x} = 1, \quad x_i \in [-1,1]
$$

**Q**: What is the solution to this relaxed problem?

---

## Solution via Eigenvalue Problem

Consider the eigenvalue problem $\mathbf{L} \mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}$. By multiplying both sides by $\mathbf{x}^\top$:

$$
\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{L} \mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{x} = \lambda
$$

(using $||\mathbf{x}|| = 1$). The left-hand side is exactly the objective we want to minimize!

The solution is the eigenvector corresponding to the **smallest eigenvalue** 😉...

---

## Confirmation Exercise

> The solution is the eigenvector corresponding to the smallest eigenvalue 😉...

```python
import numpy as np
import igraph as ig
A = ig.Graph.Famous("Zachary").get_adjacency_sparse()  # Load
```

Compute the combinatorial Laplacian $\mathbf{L} = \mathbf{D} - \mathbf{A}$ where $\mathbf{D}$ is the degree matrix.

**Task 1**: Compute the smallest eigenvalues and eigenvectors of $\mathbf{L}$ and plot the eigenvector.

**Task 2**: Compute the second smallest eigenvector and plot it.

---

## Issue with the "Best" Solution

> ~~The solution is the eigenvector corresponding to the smallest eigenvalue 😉~~

The eigenvector corresponding to the smallest eigenvalue is parallel to the all-ones vector:

$$
\mathbf{x}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \end{bmatrix}^\top
$$

This **violates** the zero mean constraint $\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{1} = 0$.

The second smallest eigenvector is orthogonal to $\mathbf{x}_1$ (eigenvectors are orthogonal to each other). So the true solution is the eigenvector corresponding to the **second smallest eigenvalue**!

The second, third, ... smallest eigenvectors can be used to get a $k$-way partition of the network.

---

## Normalized Cut

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Let us derive another spectral embedding method based on the normalized cut:

$$
\text{NC}(Q,S) = \frac{\text{cut}(Q,S)}{\text{vol}(Q)} + \frac{\text{cut}(Q,S)}{\text{vol}(S)}
$$

where $\text{vol}(Q) = \sum_{i \in Q} k_{i}$ and $\text{vol}(S) = \sum_{i \in S} k_{i}$.

The objective is expressed as $\mathbf{z}^\top \mathbf{L}_n \mathbf{z}$ where $\mathbf{z}$ is:

$$
z_i = \begin{cases} \sqrt{\frac{\text{vol}(S)}{\text{vol}(Q)}} & \text{if } i \in Q \\
-\sqrt{\frac{\text{vol}(Q)}{\text{vol}(S)}} & \text{if } i \in S
\end{cases}
$$
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
And $\mathbf{L}_n$ is the **normalized Laplacian**:

$$
\mathbf{L}_n = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{D}^{-1/2} \mathbf{A}\mathbf{D}^{-1/2}
$$

Prove it!
:::
:::

---

## Laplacian Eigenmap 🔄

**Objective**: Position connected nodes close together:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{u}} \sum_{i,j} A_{ij} (\mathbf{u}_i - \mathbf{u}_j)^2
$$

**Q**: What is the solution to this optimization problem?

---

# Neural Embedding Methods

![](figs/neural-net.png){fig-align="center"}

---

## How to Apply Neural Networks to Embedding?

1. Run random walks on the graph.
2. Treat the walks as sentences.
3. Apply neural networks to predict temporal correlations between "words" (nodes).

![](figs/random-walk-idea.png){fig-align="center" width="80%"}

**DeepWalk & node2vec**: Use *word2vec* to learn node embeddings from random walks.

::: {.callout-note}
This is one way. Another popular approach is to use *convolution* inspired from image processing.
:::

---

## Pen and Paper Exercise

[Pen and Paper Exercise](https://skojaku.github.io/adv-net-sci/_downloads/bda4489e6c047dd517f24d5dbdbcae89/exercise.pdf)

---

## CBOW Model

![](figs/word2vec-cbow.png){fig-align="center"}

---

## Skipgram

![](figs/word2vec-skipgram.png){fig-align="center"}

---

## node2vec 📝

![](figs/node2vec.png){fig-align="center" width="80%"}

node2vec learns multi-step transition probabilities of random walks. High probability of co-occurrence corresponds to closeness in the embedding space.

::: {.callout-note}
Precisely speaking, this is not an accurate model description. node2vec is trained on a biased algorithm. Consequently, two frequently co-visited nodes are *not* always embedded closely. See [this paper](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.5555/3540261.3542110).
:::

---

## node2vec Random Walks

**Biased Random Walk**:

$$
P(\text{next}|\text{current}, \text{previous})
\propto\begin{cases}
\frac{1}{p} & \text{Return to previous} \\
1 & \text{Move to neighbor} \\
\frac{1}{q} & \text{Explore further}
\end{cases}
$$

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
**Parameters**:

- $p$: Return parameter (lower = more backtracking)
- $q$: Out-degree parameter (lower = more exploration)

These control whether the walker moves *away* from the previous node or *stays locally*.
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](figs/node2vec-random-walk.png)
:::
:::

---

## Example: Les Misérables Network 📚

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="30%"}
Complementary visualizations of the Les Misérables co-appearance network generated by node2vec. Label colors reflect homophily (top) and structural equivalence (bottom).
:::
::: {.column width="70%"}
![](figs/node2vec-cp-coms.png)
:::
:::
